pr.title: Match & link ftcmdscript, clean up & document ftCo_0A01
pr.author: r-burns

summary: Split the old ftCommon/ftCo_0B3E region into a matched ftcmdscript unit and a still-nonmatching ftcpuattack unit, then cleaned up CPU command script usage in ftCo_0A01 by replacing raw numeric command bytes with a documented CPUCommand enum and better x1A88 field names. The PR linked ftcmdscript as Matching, added headers/prototypes, adjusted splits and symbols, and documented several common motion/submotion names. decomp.dev reported +3868 matched bytes, +3244 linked bytes, and 11 new matches, chiefly in main/melee/ft/ftcmdscript.
what_changed: Replaced the old split entry for melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_0B3E.c with two units: melee/ft/ftcmdscript.c at text 0x800B3E04-0x800B4AB0 and melee/ft/ftcpuattack.c at text 0x800B4AB0-0x800BBB8C.
Marked melee/ft/ftcmdscript.c as Matching in configure.py while keeping melee/ft/ftcpuattack.c NonMatching.
Added ftcmdscript.c containing the command script interpreter and helpers: ftCo_800B3E04, ftCo_800B462C, ftCo_800B463C, ftCo_800B46B8, ftCo_800B4778, ftCo_800B4880, ftCo_800B49F4, and ftCo_800B4A78.

Added ftcmdscript.h with the CPUCommand enum, command argument-range sentinels CpuCmd_ZeroArgEnd and CpuCmd_OneArgEnd, a STATIC_ASSERT that commands fit in u8, and prototypes for the matched command-script functions.
Moved remaining CPU attack code into new ftcpuattack.c/ftcpuattack.h, with most functions still as nonmatching stubs plus matched bodies such as ftCo_800B92D4 and ftCo_800B9F6C per the bot report.
Removed ftCo_0B3E.c and ftCo_0B3E.h and updated includes from "ftCo_0B3E.h" to <melee/ft/ftcmdscript.h> where the command script API is used.

Rewrote many ftCo_0A01 CPU command script builders from raw bytes such as 0x80, 0x81, 0x8E, and 0x7F to named enum constants such as CpuCmd_SetLstickX, CpuCmd_SetLstickY, CpuCmd_WaitFor, and CpuCmd_Done.

Renamed/documented fields in Fighter_x1A88_t: x4/x5/x6/x7 became lstickX/lstickY/cstickX/cstickY; x8/x9 became ltrigger/rtrigger; x10 became level; x44C/x450/x454/x554 became command_duration/csP/buffer/write_pos.
Updated users of fp->x1A88.x10 to fp->x1A88.level in fighter, Master Hand, Crazy Hand, and ftCo_0A01 code.
Changed symbols.txt labels for jump-table-like data from ftCo_803C5AF4 and ftCo_803C5DD4 to jtbl_803C5AF4 and jtbl_803C5DD4, and corrected ftCo_804D3B68 size from 0x8 to 0x2.

Added inline documentation for common motion/submotion enum values: ftCo_MS_Furafura as Dazed, ftCo_MS_Ottotto as teeter, ftCo_MS_OttottoWait as teeter wait, and corresponding ftCo_SM_Ottotto entries.
Added comments in ftcmdscript.c describing command duration semantics, buffer bounds assertions, writing commands, running scripts from the .dat command table, and resetting command input state.
smart_moves: Separated a broad, anonymous ftCo_0B3E compilation unit into semantically named files: ftcmdscript for the matched interpreter/writer and ftcpuattack for the remaining CPU attack behavior. This made it possible to link the matched subset without forcing the entire original region to match.

Converted numeric command byte literals to a typed CPUCommand enum before doing larger cleanups in ftCo_0A01. This preserved the byte-level script structure while making intent searchable and reviewable.
Kept command byte size honest with STATIC_ASSERT(CpuCmd_Count <= U8_MAX), matching the interpreter's u8/s8 command stream representation.
Used CpuCmd_ZeroArgEnd and CpuCmd_OneArgEnd sentinels to encode command arity in ftCo_800B4880 when copying command scripts from the loaded .dat table, avoiding hardcoded numeric threshold checks.
Documented struct fields at their offsets rather than only renaming local uses, which helps later decompilers avoid reintroducing x4/x10/x554-style guesses.
Preserved and exposed safety checks around command script pointers and buffer overflow: assertions for csP outside buffer and write_pos beyond buffer size.
Renamed obvious jump-table symbols to jtbl_* instead of leaving them as generic ftCo_* data objects, improving symbol semantics without claiming a higher-level name.
decomp_lessons: When a nonmatching monolithic split contains a fully matchable subsystem, split the object boundaries in splits.txt and configure.py so the matched subsystem can be linked independently.
For bytecode/interpreter-like systems, first name the opcode enum and command arity rules; downstream callsites become far easier to clean up while retaining exact emitted bytes.
Raw command values often encode signed stick magnitudes. Use s8 field names and signed literals such as -0x7F when the underlying behavior depends on signed controller axes.

Renaming struct fields from offsets to behavioral names can unlock clearer matches across many files, but every cross-file user must be updated consistently, including boss initialization code and generic fighter helpers.
Data symbols should reflect their actual role: use jtbl_* for jump tables and correct object sizes in symbols.txt when splits reveal smaller strings or table boundaries.

A decomp.dev report may show broken matches in a deleted/renamed old unit when code is moved to new split units; interpret those alongside new matches in the replacement units rather than treating them as functional regressions.
naming_conventions: CPU command opcodes use the CpuCmd_ prefix, e.g. CpuCmd_PressA, CpuCmd_ReleaseA, CpuCmd_SetLstickX, CpuCmd_WaitFor, CpuCmd_Done.
Command arity markers were named CpuCmd_ZeroArgEnd and CpuCmd_OneArgEnd, making threshold comparisons self-documenting.
Controller state fields in Fighter_x1A88_t use direct controller-style names: lstickX, lstickY, cstickX, cstickY, ltrigger, rtrigger.
CPU difficulty is named level with documentation "CPU level, 0-9".
Command script buffer management fields are named command_duration, csP, buffer, and write_pos.
Jump-table data labels use jtbl_803C.... rather than generic ftCo_803C.... when the data is identified as a jump table.
Motion/submotion comments preserve the original enum names while adding gameplay terms as Doxygen trailing comments, e.g. ftCo_MS_Ottotto ///< teeter.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Split the original text/data/sdata/sdata2 ranges for ftCo_0B3E into ftcmdscript and ftcpuattack so the command-script code could be compiled and linked as a matching unit.
Added #pragma dont_inline around part of ftCo_0A01 cleanup where present in the diff, indicating inline control was used to preserve matching in sensitive recovery/CPU behavior code.
Kept helper wrappers ftCo_800B46B8 and ftCo_800B4778 as small command-writing routines instead of inlining command writes at every callsite, preserving the original call structure.
Used explicit math library calls and lb_8000D008 for stick direction commands toward destinations/fighters, matching the interpreter's original trigonometric behavior.
Maintained buffer pointer assertions and command duration early returns in ftCo_800B3E04, matching control flow around script execution rather than simplifying it away.
Adjusted symbols and split boundaries together, including jtbl labels and sdata string sizing, to keep linker layout aligned after moving code to new files.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the provided slice.
The only comment was the decomp.dev bot report: matched code increased by 3868 bytes (+0.10%) and linked code by 3244 bytes (+0.08%).

decomp.dev listed 11 new matches, including ftCo_800B3E04, ftCo_800B4880, ftCo_800B4778, ftCo_800B46B8, ftCo_800B49F4, ftCo_800B463C, ftCo_800B4A78 in ftcmdscript, ftCo_800B92D4 and ftCo_800B9F6C in ftcpuattack, and ftCo_800A96B8 in ftCo_0A01.
decomp.dev listed 3 broken matches and 2 regressions in the old ftCo_0B3E unit, but these correspond to functions moved out of that removed split into ftcmdscript/ftcpuattack in the diff.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that finish matching melee/ft/ftcpuattack.c after the ftcmdscript split.
Search for remaining raw ftCo_800B463C or ftCo_800B46B8 calls using numeric command literals that should use CpuCmd_* names.
Audit Fighter_x1A88_t fields still named x14, x18, x1C, x44, x54, x7C, and x448 for similar documentation opportunities.
Check whether CpuCmd_Unk0x93 was later renamed after its scenario ID behavior was confirmed.
Look for later symbol cleanup around ftCo_803C6108 and remaining ftCo_804D3B70-style sdata strings.
